skill.name: zapier-workflows
skill.description: Document Zapier MCP tool preferences, store webhook-triggered Zap details, and guide Claude through repeatable automation workflows.
skill.summary: Run and document repeatable Zapier workflows from Claude Code.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: The static scan reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from markdown examples, template placeholders, and Zapier setup instructions. Real risks remain because the skill intentionally stores webhook URLs in plaintext, tells the assistant to use Bash curl for external POST requests, and persists learned workflow instructions in editable files.

medium_findings.title: Webhook URLs Stored as Plaintext Secrets
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly stores webhook URLs and warns that those URLs contain authentication tokens. Exposure would allow another party to trigger the user's Zaps.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly states that webhook URLs contain authentication tokens and are stored in plain text. This is an intended design choice, not confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Webhook Execution Through Bash Curl
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to use Bash with curl to POST to webhook URLs. This can trigger external automation and should require user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicit and operationally powerful, but it matches the skill's stated Zapier automation purpose. No evidence found of hidden destinations or covert exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Self-Modification of Workflow Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill tells Claude to edit its own reference files when users teach new Zaps, patterns, or preferences. This can persist unsafe workflow instructions if not reviewed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The self-learning behavior is clearly documented and useful for the skill. The risk depends on future user-provided content, so there is no confirmed malicious payload in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto and C2 Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-risk weak crypto and C2 labels appear to match ordinary documentation text, placeholders, and the word digest. No evidence found of cryptographic operations or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown metadata, examples, or templates. I did not find code that computes hashes, weak encryption, beaconing, or hidden command channels.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory Access Is Installation Guidance
low_findings.description: The hidden path findings refer to Claude skill installation locations and gitignore advice. They do not access files by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced paths are shown as user-facing setup guidance. The actual filesystem concern is limited to intentional self-editing of skill reference files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Expected Zapier Setup References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are Zapier MCP setup links, placeholder webhook examples, or a public repository link. They are visible documentation, not hidden exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are displayed in setup or troubleshooting sections. Network activity is still expected for Zapier use, but the reviewed URLs are not covert.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Curl Posts to Webhook URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill's execution pattern relies on Bash and curl to trigger Zapier webhooks. This is powerful automation and should be treated as an external action.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly call for curl POST requests. The risk is contextual because the destinations are user-configured Zapier webhooks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext Secret Reference Files
dangerous_patterns.description: Webhook URLs are documented in editable markdown reference files, so secret leakage is possible if files are shared or committed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill documents this risk directly and provides mitigation advice. No actual live webhook token was found in the template.

content.user_title: Automate Zapier Workflows from Claude Code
content.value_statement: Zapier automations can be hard to remember and trigger consistently from chat. This skill documents workflows, tool preferences, and webhook patterns so Claude can run repeatable automations.
actual_capabilities: Documents Zapier webhook-triggered Zaps in a reusable reference file.
Guides Claude to trigger documented Zaps with Bash curl POST requests.
Records preferred Zapier MCP tools and when to use them.
Supports repeatable research, spreadsheet, digest, expense, and lead tracking workflows.
Prompts users to save successful multi-tool patterns for future sessions.
limitations: Requires a Zapier account and configured Zapier MCP tools or webhook URLs.
Stores webhook URLs in plaintext unless the user changes the storage approach.
Does not verify that a Zap ran successfully beyond available tool or webhook feedback.
Can become stale if Zap names, URLs, or connected apps change in Zapier.

use_cases.title: Run Team Operations Zaps
use_cases.description: Trigger documented daily briefings, lead routing, or notification workflows from a natural language request.

use_cases.title: Save Research Results
use_cases.description: Use preferred research tools, summarize findings, and store structured notes in a chosen destination.

use_cases.title: Track Personal Admin Tasks
use_cases.description: Capture expenses, reminders, and recurring records through repeatable Zapier and Google Sheets workflows.

prompt_templates.title: List Available Workflows
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the Zapier workflows and MCP patterns this skill currently knows about.

prompt_templates.title: Trigger a Known Zap
prompt_templates.prompt: Run my documented daily digest Zap and tell me what action you triggered.

prompt_templates.title: Document a New Webhook Zap
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a new Zapier webhook workflow. Help me record its purpose, trigger phrases, expected output, and safety notes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Tool Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a repeatable Zapier MCP pattern for researching a company, summarizing results, and saving them to my preferred sheet.

output_examples.output: Claude checks the saved Zap reference, identifies the matching webhook workflow, triggers it, and confirms the expected delivery channel.

output_examples.input: Remember that expense tracking should use my Personal Budget sheet.
output_examples.output: Claude updates the workflow preference so future expense entries use the named sheet and format.

output_examples.input: Help me add a Zap for new sales leads.
output_examples.output: Claude asks for the webhook URL, trigger phrases, workflow steps, output destination, and safety notes before saving the workflow.

best_practices: Use placeholder webhook URLs in shared copies of the skill.
Confirm every external webhook trigger before running it.
Review saved workflow references after adding new automation patterns.
anti_patterns: Do not commit real webhook URLs to public repositories.
Do not save one-off requests as permanent workflow patterns.
Do not trigger workflows when the destination or expected effect is unclear.

faq.question: Does this skill require Zapier MCP?
faq.answer: No. It can use webhook-triggered Zaps, Zapier MCP tools, or both.

faq.question: Can it create Zaps inside Zapier?
faq.answer: No. It helps document and trigger workflows that are already created in Zapier.

faq.question: Where are workflow details stored?
faq.answer: They are stored in markdown reference files inside the skill directory.

faq.question: Are webhook URLs treated as secrets?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill warns that webhook URLs can trigger Zaps and should not be shared.

faq.question: Can Claude learn new workflow preferences?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill instructs Claude to update reference files when users approve new patterns.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing?
faq.answer: Users should remove live webhook URLs, private sheet names, and sensitive workflow details.
